infoAlert.title: About the Goal Calculator
infoAlert.descriptionWithBodyFat: This calculator estimates your target weight based on desired body fat % and preserved lean mass.
infoAlert.descriptionWithoutBodyFat: This calculator helps you set weight goals and calculate timelines. Add body fat % to your profile for more detailed calculations.
infoAlert.disclaimer: The timeline is an estimate - actual results may vary depending on training, diet and individual factors.

bmi.disclaimer: NOTE: BMI does not take muscle mass, bone density, overall body composition or sex differences into account. Therefore it should not be a sole guiding measurement to a better health — use body fat % as a complement.

noProfile.title: No active profile
noProfile.description: You need a profile to use the genetic muscle potential tool.

header.title: Genetic Muscle Potential
header.description: Calculate your maximum muscle mass based on height and body structure

maleOnly.title: Genetic Muscle Potential is for men only
maleOnly.description: These models and frameworks (Martin Berkhan's model, Casey Butt's model, Alan Aragon's framework and Lyle McDonald's framework) are all developed and validated for men. They do not account for women's physiology and hormonal differences, meaning the results would not be accurate or useful for women.

addBodyFatTip.title: Tip: Add body fat percentage
addBodyFatTip.description: To see your current progress towards genetic potential and how many kg of lean mass you have left to build, we recommend entering your body fat percentage in your profile. The calculations work without this, but you get more insightful information with body fat percentage.
addBodyFatTip.link: Go to Profile to enter body fat percentage

infoCard.title: About Genetic Muscle Potential
infoCard.description: These models estimate your maximum natural muscle mass. Results vary between formulas depending on what factors they consider. No formula is 100% accurate, but they provide a useful guideline for realistic goals.

geneticPotential.exceedingPotential: Above theoretical max for lean mass:
geneticPotential.capacityAtCurrentBF: Estimated maximum capacity at current {{bf}}% body fat

caseyButtInfo.para1: Casey Butt is a researcher (PhD) and powerlifter who published a comprehensive empirical model in the 2000s for estimating genetic muscle potential in natural athletes. The model is based on analysis of historical data from natural bodybuilders and strength athletes over several decades, particularly from periods when performance-enhancing drugs were uncommon.
caseyButtInfo.para2: Unlike simpler height- and weight-based formulas, Butt's model accounts for individual skeletal structure through wrist and ankle circumference measurements. These are used to estimate frame size and muscle potential. The model also provides separate calculations for maximum body measurements (e.g. arms, chest, thighs) at genetic potential.
caseyButtInfo.para3: The model is empirical and observation-based, not a formally scientifically validated study, but is often used as a reference for realistic upper limits of natural muscle development, not as a statistical average model.
caseyButtInfo.step1Vars: H = height (inches), W = wrist (inches), A = ankle (inches), BF% = body fat
caseyButtInfo.easygainerVars: W = wrist, A = ankle, H = height (inches)
caseyButtInfo.source: Source: Butt, C. "Your Muscular Potential: How to Predict Your Maximum Muscular Bodyweight and Measurements" weightrainer.net (originally published early 2000s, updated 2009)
caseyButtInfo.ambiguityNote: There is ambiguity with this model. Casey Butt does not strictly specify which body fat percentage to use, but describes: "%bf = The body fat percentage at which you want to predict your maximum lean body mass". This leaves it open to interpretation and the model can therefore be used in two different ways:
caseyButtInfo.alternativesLabel:
Option 1: Standardised genetic reference
Option 2: State-dependent lean body mass

caseyButtInputs.sectionTitle: Circumference measurements for the Casey Butt model
caseyButtInputs.descWithData: These measurements are used to calculate skeletal structure and muscle potential. Values are pre-filled from your active measurement card.
caseyButtInputs.descWithoutData: These measurements are used to calculate skeletal structure and muscle potential. Enter your measurements manually or save them in body composition.
caseyButtInputs.wristInfoDesc: Measured on the palm side of the styloid processes, where the forearm is narrowest.

caseyButtMethod.sectionTitle: Choose calculation method for the Casey Butt model
caseyButtMethod.standardDesc: Calculates MLBM using 10% body fat as a reference, then converted to your actual body fat percentage. Comparable between individuals and based on Casey Butt's original data.
caseyButtMethod.personalizedDesc: Shows how much lean body mass your body can carry at your current body fat percentage, including fluids and glycogen.
caseyButtMethod.missingBodyFat: ⚠️ Body fat missing — using 10% as fallback

standardModal.title: Standardised genetic reference
standardModal.description: This method calculates your maximum genetic potential in two steps:
standardModal.step1: MLBM (Maximum Lean Body Mass) is calculated using 10% body fat as a standardised reference point
standardModal.step2Label: converted to your actual body fat percentage
standardModal.step2Post: to show your maximum weight at your current body fat
standardModal.step3: Casey Butt's analysis was based on approximately 300 drug-free bodybuilding champions and strength athletes from 1947–2010. These athletes were in a lean, stable condition (~8–10% body fat), which is why 10% is used as the reference point for the MLBM calculation.
standardModal.step4: By using 10% as a fixed reference in the MLBM formula, but then converting to your actual body fat percentage, you get a genetically meaningful result that is also relevant to your current condition.
standardModal.pro1: Genetically meaningful — MLBM based on a standardised reference
standardModal.pro2: Comparable between individuals — everyone uses the same 10% reference
standardModal.pro4: Relevant to your current condition — shows weight at your actual body fat
standardModal.con1: May be harder to understand (two-step calculation)
standardModal.con2: Requires knowledge of your body fat percentage for best results

personalizedModal.title: State-dependent lean body mass
personalizedModal.para1: The result will show how much lean body mass your body can carry in this condition. It also includes fluids, glycogen and other non-permanent components.
personalizedModal.para2: Here the user's %BF is used directly in MLBM (maximum lean body mass). MBW (maximum body weight) and MBBW (maximum bulked body weight) are then calculated from this.
personalizedModal.pro1: Fully correct according to the formula
personalizedModal.con1: MLBM becomes state-dependent (MLBM increases when %BF increases)
personalizedModal.con3: Less suitable as a genetic ceiling

berkhanInfo.para1: Martin Berkhan is the founder of the Leangains method and an early advocate of intermittent fasting in the fitness world. In the article "Maximum Muscular Potential of Drug-Free Athletes" he presents a simple model for estimating maximum muscle mass in natural (drug-free) athletes.
berkhanInfo.para2: The model is based on observations of elite bodybuilders and competitive athletes at very low body fat percentages, primarily around 5–6% for men. Berkhan suggests that maximum competition weight in peak condition can be estimated using the formula:
berkhanInfo.para3: He emphasises that this value represents an upper practical limit for natural athletes in extreme competition shape, rather than an average or a guaranteed goal. Berkhan also notes that individual genetics, body structure and height may justify minor adjustments around this estimate (typically ±2–4 kg).
berkhanInfo.para4: The model is empirical and based on practical observations, not formal scientific validation, and should therefore be seen as a rough guideline rather than an exact physiological law.
berkhanInfo.source: Source: Berkhan, M. "Maximum Muscular Potential of Drug-Free Athletes" leangains.com (2008-2010) (updated 31 Dec 2010)

caseyButtMissing.title: Circumference measurements required for the Casey Butt model
caseyButtMissing.description: To calculate your results with Casey Butt's model you need to enter circumference measurements for wrist and ankle in the fields above. These measurements can either be pre-filled from your active measurement card in Body Composition, or you can enter them manually.

resultCard.targetWeightsTitle: Estimated maximum genetic potential for body weight
resultCard.targetWeightsSubtitle: Max weight at different body fat percentages

lyleMcDonald.para1: Lyle McDonald is an author and analyst in nutrition and strength training who since the late 1990s has published extensive material on muscle building, energy balance and training adaptation. In the article "What's My Genetic Muscular Potential?" he discusses how muscle growth gradually slows with increased training age and approaches an individual genetic upper limit.
lyleMcDonald.para2: McDonald's reasoning is based on a synthesis of scientific literature, practical observations and comparisons between beginners, intermediates and advanced trainees. Unlike quantitative models for maximum muscle mass, he presents no specific calculation procedure, but offers a theoretical framework for understanding likely development trajectories and realistic expectations over time.
lyleMcDonald.source: Source: McDonald, L. What's My Genetic Muscular Potential? BodyRecomposition.com
lyleMcDonald.tableSubtitle: Based on "proper" training with progressive overload
lyleMcDonald.footNote: Note: These values assume correct training, nutrition and recovery

alanAragon.para1: Alan Aragon is a nutritionist and coach, and founder of Alan Aragon's Research Review (AARR). In his publications, including the article "Girth Control: The Science of Fat Loss and Muscle Gain" (2007), he discusses how muscle building and body composition change in relation to training level, experience and time.
alanAragon.para2: Aragon's perspective focuses on realistic expectations and adaptation rates rather than establishing absolute genetic limits. He describes how the potential for muscle gain is greatest in beginners and successively decreases in more advanced trainees. The work thus functions as a conceptual framework for time-dependent muscle growth, not as a numerical model for maximum muscle mass.
alanAragon.source: Source: Aragon, A. Girth Control: The Science of Fat Loss and Muscle Gain (2007), Alan Aragon's Research Review
alanAragon.tableSubtitle: Percentage of total body weight per month
alanAragon.footNote: Note: Assess for yourself where you are based on your actual training experience

bodyType.upperHardDesc: Thinner skeletal structure — requires more focus for upper body development
bodyType.upperEasyDesc: Thicker skeletal structure — easier to build upper body muscles
bodyType.lowerHardDesc: Thinner skeletal structure — requires more focus for lower body development
bodyType.lowerEasyDesc: Thicker skeletal structure — easier to build lower body muscles

maxMeasurements.chestInfoDesc: Raise your arms and place the tape measure around the upper part of the torso, below the armpits, before lowering the arms to the sides. Chest circumference is measured horizontally at exhalation and in a relaxed state.
maxMeasurements.bicepsInfoDesc: Measured in a flexed position at the largest circumference, with the arm raised forward and elbow at 45°.
maxMeasurements.forearmsInfoDesc: With a clenched fist and arm extended, measure the circumference at the largest point, usually closer to the elbow.
maxMeasurements.neckInfoDesc:
According to Topend Sports: Immediately above the Adam's apple.
According to Casey Butt: Just below the Adam's apple, at the narrowest point.

The person should keep their head upright and look straight ahead.
maxMeasurements.thighsInfoDesc: Measured at the midpoint of the outer thigh, halfway between the greater trochanter and the lateral tibial condyle.
maxMeasurements.calvesInfoDesc: Measured at the largest circumference, with muscles relaxed.

palFields.customPALHint: Enter your own PAL value if you know it
palFields.customPALWarning: Unusual PAL value — normal range is 1.0–2.5

energyGoalTable.title: Energy goal - Overview
energyGoalTable.invalidTdee: Invalid TDEE value. Please calculate your TDEE first.
energyGoalTable.maintainDesc: For weight stabilisation and maintenance of current weight
energyGoalTable.gainDesc: For muscle building and weight gain · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
energyGoalTable.lossCautiousDesc: Slow weight loss, best for preserving muscle mass · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
energyGoalTable.lossNormalDesc: Balanced weight loss with good energy levels · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
energyGoalTable.lossAggressiveDesc: Rapid weight loss, short periods only · ~{{kgMin}}–{{kgMax}} kg/week
